planting.note: Plant container stock or root divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in zone 7–11; Honduran Sarsaparilla is a thorny, woody, climbing vine native to the humid forests of Central America — Honduras, Mexico, Jamaica, and Guatemala — whose aromatic, starchy rhizomes are the authentic source of the flavoring used in traditional root beer and the original sarsaparilla beverages, and a valued traditional medicine for joint pain and skin conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade with trellis or other support for climbing; Smilax ornata is adaptable to a range of conditions including full sun with consistent moisture and deep shade — mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and supply organic matter to the root zone. Smilax ornata (Honduran Sarsaparilla, Zarzaparilla) is a thorny, woody, perennial climbing vine in the Smilacaceae family native to humid tropical forests, forest edges, and riparian corridors of Central America — particularly Honduras, Guatemala, Mexico (Veracruz and Chiapas), Cuba, and Jamaica — where it climbs through the understorey using tendrils. The long, aromatic, reddish-brown rhizomes are the authentic source of 'true' sarsaparilla flavoring — the complex, vanilla-anise-wintergreen root flavor historically used in 19th-century American sarsaparilla beverages and root beer before artificial flavors displaced it. Medicinally, sarsaparilla root was a major export from Central America and the Caribbean to European pharmaceutical markets in the 16th–19th centuries for treatment of syphilis, psoriasis, rheumatism, and as a blood-purifying tonic. Modern phytochemical research confirms anti-inflammatory steroidal saponins (sarsasaponin, smilasaponin) in the rhizomes. The rhizomes are harvested by carefully digging, removing a portion, and replanting the crown; the vine regenerates. Excellent shade-tolerant medicinal vine for forest-garden and woodland-garden settings in zones 7–11.
sources: Morton, J. — Atlas of Medicinal Plants of Middle America

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, low-fertility soil in full sun in zone 8–11; Devil's Claw is a low-growing, trailing perennial native to the Kalahari Desert and Namib-Karoo dryland of southern Africa, grown for its secondary storage tubers — one of the most widely sold botanical medicines in Europe for osteoarthritis, lower back pain, and inflammation — and characterized by spectacular claw-shaped, woody seed pods evolved for dispersal by large mammals.

directions: Direct sow in very well-drained, sandy to gravelly, low-fertility soil in full sun; Harpagophytum procumbens is a dryland plant that requires excellent drainage and tolerates severe drought — overwatering or clay soils cause root rot; grow in raised beds or sandy garden beds with no supplemental fertilizer. Harpagophytum procumbens (Devil's Claw, Grapple Plant, or Duiwelsklauw) is a low-growing, trailing, tender perennial in the Pedaliaceae family native to the Kalahari Desert, Namibian desert, and Karoo drylands of Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa — an extraordinarily specialized dryland plant adapted to survival in some of the harshest desert soils in the world. The plant is named for its grotesque, claw-covered, woody seed pods — each pod festooned with multiple long, hooked claws designed to attach to the hooves and legs of large mammals (springbok, wildebeest, zebra) for long-distance dispersal across the desert. The secondary storage tubers (secondary roots) are the medicinal part — harvested commercially in Namibia and Botswana — containing harpagoside, 8-p-coumaroylharpagide, and related iridoid glycosides with well-documented anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. Devil's Claw root extract is one of the top three best-selling botanical medicines in Germany and France, prescribed for osteoarthritis, lower back pain, and musculoskeletal inflammation. Wild harvesting is regulated; cultivated production increasingly supplies European pharmaceutical markets.
sources: WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants — Harpagophytum procumbens

subcategory: Tropical ornamental and industrial oil plant

planting.note: WARNING: ALL parts of Ricinus communis are EXTREMELY TOXIC — the seeds contain ricin, one of the most potent naturally occurring toxins known. Keep away from children and livestock; wear gloves when handling seeds and wash hands thoroughly. Plant seeds directly outdoors after last frost in full sun in well-drained, fertile to average soil; Castor Bean is a fast-growing tropical ornamental with extraordinarily large, bold, star-shaped leaves in green, bronze, or deep red — one of the most dramatic foliage plants available for large seasonal tropical landscape displays.

directions: SAFETY WARNING: All parts of Ricinus communis — especially seeds — are extremely toxic due to ricin content; never plant where children or livestock have access; wear gloves when handling seeds and pods and wash hands immediately after contact. Direct sow 2 large seeds 1–2 inches deep in full sun after last frost in well-drained, fertile, loamy soil; thin to the strongest seedling. Ricinus communis (Castor Bean or Castor Oil Plant) is a fast-growing, large, tropical perennial or annual shrub in the Euphorbiaceae family native to the eastern tropical African highlands and horn of Africa, now naturalized throughout tropical and subtropical regions worldwide. As a landscape ornamental, Castor Bean is unsurpassed for bold, tropical foliage effect: the huge, deeply-lobed, star-shaped leaves — reaching 1–3 feet across — come in deep bronze, dark red-maroon, or bright green depending on cultivar, held on long stalks atop rapidly growing plants that can reach 6–15 feet in a single season. The industrial importance is enormous: castor oil (cold-pressed from the seeds after ricin deactivation) is one of the most commercially important specialty vegetable oils — valued as a lubricant, biodiesel feedstock, cosmetic emollient, nylon-11 precursor, and traditional purgative medicine. The seeds contain ricin — a biological toxin in the RIP (ribosome-inactivating protein) class — one of the most acutely toxic natural substances known; as few as 4–8 seeds can be lethal to an adult if chewed. All handling of seeds and seed pods should be done with gloves.
sources: Poison Control Centers — Ricinus communis Toxicity
FAO Industrial Crops — Castor Oil Ricinus communis

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, deeply cultivated soil in full sun in zone 5–9; Madder is a sprawling, climbing perennial native to the eastern Mediterranean and Western Asia that has been cultivated since ancient times as the primary source of red natural dye — the rose-to-scarlet-to-orange-red pigment alizarin and purpurin extracted from the roots colored textiles, rugs, and art from ancient Egypt through the 19th century until synthetic dyes replaced it.

directions: Direct sow in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, deeply loosened soil in full sun; Rubia tinctorum tolerates drought once established and benefits from calcium-rich soils — add lime to acidic soils and avoid waterlogging. Rubia tinctorum (Madder or Dyer's Madder) is a rambling, rhizomatous, rough-stemmed perennial climbing herb in the Rubiaceae (coffee) family native to the eastern Mediterranean, Turkey, and Central Asia, now widely naturalized across Europe and cultivated by natural dyers worldwide. For more than 5,000 years — documented in Egyptian tomb textiles dating to 1500 BCE, Indian trade records, and Islamic textile traditions — Madder root was the primary source of red, orange, and coral dyes for textiles, wool, silk, and cotton worldwide. The roots contain alizarin (1,2-dihydroxyanthraquinone) and purpurin, which when mordanted with alum produce clear reds, with iron produce purples and browns, and with other mordants produce a full spectrum from coral to brick-red to burgundy. Madder root cultivation was an enormous agricultural industry in the Netherlands, France, Turkey, and India from the medieval period until the synthesis of alizarin from coal tar by Perkin in 1869 collapsed the natural dye industry almost overnight. Today Madder is grown by natural dyers, craft textile artists, and historical recreationists who value plant-based dyes. The plants need 2–3 years to produce roots of harvestable size.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Rubia tinctorum

planting.note: Plant root divisions or crowns in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in zone 7–11; Ramie is a large, rhizomatous perennial nettle-family plant native to subtropical China, grown since ancient times as the primary source of one of the world's longest, strongest, and most lustrous bast fibers — used for thousands of years in Chinese textiles, mummies' wrapping cloth, and now blended with linen and cotton for high-quality natural fabric.

directions: Plant root divisions 2–3 inches deep in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Boehmeria nivea spreads aggressively by rhizome — contain with deep edging or grow in a dedicated plot; harvest stems when 36–50 inches tall by cutting at ground level, typically 2–4 harvests per year in warm climates. Boehmeria nivea (Ramie, China Grass, or White Ramie) is a robust, rhizomatous, herbaceous perennial in the Urticaceae (nettle) family native to subtropical to tropical East Asia — China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan, and the Indo-Chinese peninsula — where it has been cultivated for textile fiber for at least 6,000 years, making it one of the oldest cultivated fiber crops on earth. The bast fiber extracted from the long, upright stems is among the strongest, longest (individual fibers can reach 4–6 inches), and most lustrous of all natural textile fibers — stronger than flax (linen) and comparable to cotton in softness but with a silky sheen — and is used for high-quality clothing, table linens, lace, canvas, industrial fiber, and blended with cotton and silk. Processing ramie for spinning is labor-intensive (requiring degumming), which limited commercial adoption in the West, but in China, Korea, Japan, and the Philippines, Ramie clothing production is substantial. Leaves of Boehmeria nivea are also nutritious (20–27% crude protein) and fed to livestock and fish (particularly carp) in China as a high-protein fodder. An ornamental value in the large, white-undersided leaves.

subcategory: Mediterranean and Middle Eastern wild pistachio tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky to sandy soil in zone 7–10; Mt. Atlas Pistachio is a large, extremely drought-tolerant, long-lived pistachio species native to the Atlas Mountains of North Africa, Turkey, and the Middle East — the primary rootstock for commercial Pistacia vera pistachio grafts — bearing small, resinous, edible pistachio-like nuts and exceptional autumn color in cold-tolerant zone 7 climates.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic, lean, rocky or sandy soil; Pistacia atlantica is remarkably drought tolerant and thrives in conditions too harsh for most fruit trees — avoid clay, overwatering, and excess fertility, which promote lush growth at the expense of drought adaptation and cold hardiness. Pistacia atlantica (Mt. Atlas Pistachio, Mastic Pistachio, or Bene) is a large, long-lived, drought-adapted deciduous to semi-evergreen tree native to the Atlas Mountains of Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, and extending across the Middle East to Central Asia — the wild pistachio tree most widely used as the cold-hardy rootstock upon which commercial Pistacia vera (edible pistachio) varieties are grafted, conferring exceptional drought tolerance, deep-rooting habit, and disease resistance to the scions. The tree itself produces small clusters of small, pea-sized, resinous, edible nuts resembling tiny pistachios — edible and pistachio-flavored when roasted, historically used as a seasoning and oil source across the Middle East and North Africa. Pistacia atlantica is also the source of a gum-resin historically used as a primitive chewing gum and incense in the Middle East. As an ornamental landscape tree, it is exceptional: extremely long-lived, deeply drought-tolerant, with elegant pinnate foliage and spectacular orange-red-purple autumn color in cooler climates. Planted in California's pistachio-growing regions as a street and shade tree for its drought tolerance.
sources: UC Davis Department of Plant Sciences — Pistachio Rootstocks (Pistacia atlantica)
